title: Non-Uniqueness Of Locally Minimizing Clusters
speaker: Anna Skorobogatova
abstract:
Optimal bubble cluster problems concern the study of partitions of ℝn
 into a fnite collection of chambers, some with finite volume and some with infinite volume. One looks for local minimizers of interfacial area subject to volume constraints on the finite-volume chambers. The case of one infinite-volume chamber is the classical multiple bubble problem and has received much attention in recent decades, with a well-known conjecture of Sullivan predicting the existence of a unique minimizing configuration when there are not too many chambers, which has been partially verified to be true in low dimensions. 
We study a variant of the multiple bubble problem with more than one infinite-volume chamber, in particular the simplest case of 1 finite-volume chamber and 2 infinite-volume chambers. Here, Bronsard & Novack showed that uniqueness of local minimizers also holds in sufficiently low dimensions. In stark contrast, we show that uniqueness fails in a large number of dimensions n greater than or equal to 8
 and we provide some particular surprising phenomena that local minimizers can exhibit in these higher dimensions. This is based on joint work with Lia Bronsard, Robin Neumayer and Michael Novack.

proof_stages.label: cluster variational setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines locally finite-perimeter partitions with finitely many prescribed finite-volume chambers and several infinite-volume chambers, local compactly supported competitors, and the resulting CMC versus minimal interface Euler–Lagrange conditions.

proof_stages.label: known uniqueness regimes and reduction of focus
proof_stages.summary: Classical isoperimetric and multiple-bubble uniqueness results, planar classifications, and the Bronsard–Novack uniqueness theorem through ambient dimension seven motivate restricting attention to one finite-volume chamber and two infinite-volume chambers.

proof_stages.label: high-dimensional nonuniqueness theorem via singular blow-downs
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that in dimensions 8 through 700 there are local minimizers not congruent to the standard lens, with the key mechanism that blow-downs of infinite interfaces may be singular area-minimizing hypercones rather than planes.

proof_stages.label: penalized conical Dirichlet problems at large scale
proof_stages.summary: For large annuli one prescribes conical boundary data and solves a perimeter minimization problem with a small finite-chamber penalization that prevents boundary attachment while vanishing in the eventual local limit.

proof_stages.label: concentration decomposition of finite chamber mass
proof_stages.summary: The finite chamber is decomposed into uniformly finitely many nondegenerate, bounded-diameter concentrations separated from each other, isolating the sole dangerous case in which every concentration escapes to infinity looking lens-shaped.

proof_stages.label: competitor comparison excluding escaping lenses
proof_stages.summary: If all concentrations are escaping lenses, an isoperimetric concavity argument first combines them into a single lens, and a cone-specific competitor modeled on quadratic area-minimizing cones is then shown by interval-arithmetic energy comparison to be strictly cheaper.

proof_stages.label: extraction of a non-lens local minimizer
proof_stages.summary: Ruling out the enemy scenario guarantees that at least one nondegenerate concentration has a local limit whose blow-down is a singular cone, proving nonuniqueness while initially giving little finer information because the prescribed cone and the mass live on incomparable scales.

proof_stages.label: refined prescribed blow-down and non-sticking phenomena
proof_stages.summary: Subsequent work uses Hardt–Simon foliations and related energy comparisons to prescribe the blow-down cone under suitable hypotheses and to show that the infinite interface can be asymptotic to the cone without actually attaching to it, including certain non-isolated singular settings.

examples.name: classical isoperimetric ball
examples.note: The case of one finite-volume chamber and one infinite chamber is recalled as the familiar unique ball minimizer, serving as a baseline uniqueness example for the cluster problem.
examples.engagement_reason: The classical one finite/one infinite chamber case is cited as the isoperimetric problem with the ball as unique minimizer, without deriving or computing anything on the ball.

examples.name: standard multiple bubbles
examples.note: Standard m-bubbles, including the double-bubble and low-dimensional triple/quadruple-bubble pictures, illustrate the classical one-infinite-chamber uniqueness expectations.
examples.engagement_reason: Standard multiple bubbles are described as known/conjectured configurations with Voronoi/stereographic description and angle/cap facts, but the speaker does not work through a specific construction or verification.

examples.engagement_reason: Copolymer melts and the liquid-drop model are invoked only as physical motivation with a qualitative description of the energy and blow-up picture.

examples.name: planar peanut and rectangular clusters
examples.note: The peanut and rectangular planar configurations illustrate the richer catalogue of known local minimizers when finite and infinite chambers are mixed in the plane.
examples.engagement_reason: Planar peanut and rectangular clusters are listed and sketched as classified possibilities with 120-degree angles, but no property is checked or construction carried out on them.
